coredata.dc:title: Single-step synthesized carbon quantum dots from Centella asiatica hairy roots: Photoluminescent, biocompatibility, antibacterial and anticancer activity

coredata.dc:description: Medicinal plant-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are emerging as promising nanomaterials with diverse biomedical applications and favorable biocompatibility profiles. In a novel approach, this study reports for the first time the synthesis of CQDs from transgenic hairy roots of Centella asiatica, carrying the Arabidopsis thaliana squalene synthase gene (At-SS1cDNA) as a sustainable and scalable precursor source. The optical characteristics were described using UV-Vis and photoluminescence spectroscopies, while their chemical and structural properties were examined using, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), dynamic light scattering (DLS), Zeta potential analysis, X-ray diffraction (XRD), energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX), and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM). According to the results, the synthesized CQDs were uniformly dispersed spherical nanoparticles with a mean diameter of 1.07 ± 0.2 nm. XRD revealed the crystalline graphitic carbon phases, mixed with amorphous regions. FTIR analysis identified hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, and amino groups, as the most dominant functional entities present on the CQDs’ surface. The biological properties of CQDs synthesized from C. asiatica hairy roots were significantly superior, compaed to those from its methanolic and aqueous extracts. CQDs exhibited high antioxidant activity, comparable to trolax, as a standard reference. CQDs displayed superior antibacterial efficacy, particularly against Staphylococcus aureus, having an minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of 0.25 mg/mL. MTT assay showed a selectivity index of 13.87 for CQDs, indicating substantial selectivity towards cancer cells, rather than normal cell. The hemolysis test demonstrated that CQDs remained well below the 5 % cytocompatibility threshold across all concentrations, indicating high biocompatibility with minimal hemolytic effects. These findings suggest that CQDs produced sustainably from C. asiatica hairy roots, hold considerable promise for biomedical applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Medicinal plant-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are emerging as promising nanomaterials with diverse biomedical applications and favorable biocompatibility profiles. In a novel approach, this study reports for the first time the synthesis of CQDs from transgenic hairy roots of Centella asiatica, carrying the Arabidopsis thaliana squalene synthase gene (At-SS1cDNA) as a sustainable and scalable precursor source. The optical characteristics were described using UV-Vis and photoluminescence spectroscopies, while their chemical and structural properties were examined using, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), dynamic light scattering (DLS), Zeta potential analysis, X-ray diffraction (XRD), energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX), and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM). According to the results, the synthesized CQDs were uniformly dispersed spherical nanoparticles with a mean diameter of 1.07 ± 0.2 nm. XRD revealed the crystalline graphitic carbon phases, mixed with amorphous regions. FTIR analysis identified hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, and amino groups, as the most dominant functional entities present on the CQDs’ surface. The biological properties of CQDs synthesized from C. asiatica hairy roots were significantly superior, compaed to those from its methanolic and aqueous extracts. CQDs exhibited high antioxidant activity, comparable to trolax, as a standard reference. CQDs displayed superior antibacterial efficacy, particularly against Staphylococcus aureus, having an minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of 0.25 mg/mL. MTT assay showed a selectivity index of 13.87 for CQDs, indicating substantial selectivity towards cancer cells, rather than normal cell. The hemolysis test demonstrated that CQDs remained well below the 5 % cytocompatibility threshold across all concentrations, indicating high biocompatibility with minimal hemolytic effects. These findings suggest that CQDs produced sustainably from C. asiatica hairy roots, hold considerable promise for biomedical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanomaterials (CNMs), including fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphene derivatives, and carbon quantum dots (CQDs), exhibit high surface area, mechanical strength, and tunable physicochemical properties, enabling diverse applications (Ahmed et al., 2024; Azad and Chandra, 2023). Their conductivity and biocompatibility drive uses in electronics, aerospace, and biomedicine (e.g., drug delivery, diagnostics), while adsorption and catalytic capabilities support environmental remediation (Priya et al., 2024; Mahor et al., 2021). CNMs also enhance consumer products through antimicrobial/UV-protective traits and improve agriculture via nano-fertilizers and soil health strategies ( Wadhwa et al., 2019; Vernekar et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2022). Notably, CQDs (<10 nm), discovered in 2004, offer unique advantages such as low toxicity, water solubility, and stable photoluminescence, making them ideal for biomedical, photocatalytic, and energy applications (Ahmed et al., 2024; Kumar et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2004).

Herbal medicine CQDs (HM-CQDs), derived from medicinal plants, are emerging as advanced nanomaterials with potential biomedical applications, including bioimaging, biosensing, drug delivery, and DNA interactions (Ashok Kumar et al., 2023). They also exhibit therapeutic properties such as antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, anti-angiogenesis, and antimicrobial activities (Baranwal et al., 2018; Kumar et al., 2023a; Luo et al., 2021; Vibhute et al., 2023; Zeng et al., 2023). Carbon quantum dots can be produced using a variety of methods, such as laser ablation, arc discharge, microwave pyrolysis, acidic oxidation, hydrothermal, and solvothermal techniques (Cui et al., 2021; Pandya et al., 2024; Yadav et al., 2023).

The green synthesis of CQDs offers a sustainable approach using natural compounds from various sources, including citrus fruits, berries, teas, vegetables, and medicinal plants like Simarouba glauca DC, Annona squamosa L., Zingiber officinale Roscoe, Centella asiatica (L.) Urban, and Andrographis paniculata (Li et al., 2023; Naik et al., 2020; Rodríguez-Varillas et al., 2022; Thokchom et al., 2023; Vibhute et al., 2023). Phytochemicals and antioxidants in these extracts act as carbon sources and passivation agents, yielding biocompatible CQDs suitable for applications in biotechnology, biomedicine, sensing, imaging, catalysis, and environmental remediation (Bressi et al., 2023; Mathew et al., 2023).

Utilizing sustainable biomass sources, such as agricultural waste and controlled biomass production methods like plant tissue and hairy root cultures, enhances the scalability and efficiency of CQDs synthesis. Agricultural wastes, including dragon fruit peels, banana peels, sugarcane bagasse, coffee grounds, and corn straw, have been successfully used for CQDs production (Atchudan et al., 2021; Dhongde et al., 2024; Kasinathan et al., 2022; Kundu et al., 2022; Mirseyed et al., 2024; Mu et al., 2024; Zhu et al., 2023). Hairy root cultures, induced by Rhizobium rhizogenes (formerly Agrobacterium rhizogenes), offer a sustainable alternative for biomass production, ensuring genetic stability, protecting wild plant populations, and maintaining bioactive compound quality for advanced applications (Gholami et al., 2020; Ji et al., 2023; Kochan et al., 2024; Kumar et al., 2023b).

Centella asiatica, known by various names worldwide, is a medicinal herb from the Apiaceae family, primarily found in tropical and subtropical regions, including Asia, South Africa, America, Australia, and the Pacific Islands, while in Iran, it is restricted to the humid Hyrcanian region (Nav et al., 2021; Samuel et al., 2022). Overexploitation, poor cultivation practices, and seed viability issues threaten its wild populations, highlighting the need for sustainable production methods (Ganie et al., 2022; Nayar and Sastry, 1987). Botanically, C. asiatica has kidney-shaped leaves, small pink flowers, and a creeping growth habit. It has been widely used in Ayurvedic and traditional medicine due to its rich bioactive profile, including terpenoids, flavonoids, alkaloids, tannins, and phenols. Among these, pentacyclic triterpenoids—madecassoside, asiaticoside, madecassic acid, and asiatic acid, collectively known as centellosides—are the key bioactive compounds (Gallego et al., 2014; Ganie et al., 2022; Prasad et al., 2016). These compounds contribute to C. asiatica’s pharmacological properties, such as promoting wound healing through collagen synthesis, neuroprotection by enhancing memory and neuro-regeneration, and exhibiting anticancer, antidiabetic, anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, antibacterial, and antiviral activities (Li et al., 2014; Yingchun et al., 2019).

Several biotechnological techniques such as plant tissue and hairy roots cultures have been established and developed from C. asiatica for the production of centellosides so far (Alcalde et al., 2022, 2023a, 2023b; Gallego et al., 2014). Recently, a transgenic hairy root line carrying the Arabidopsis thaliana squalene synthase gene (At-SS1cDNA) from the plant has been developed by this group (Data not shown), so, the transgenic root line was employed for the synthesis of CQDs for the first time. Hairy root culture represents an effective strategy for the mass production of medicinal plant biomass and specialized metabolites (SMs) (Sonkar et al., 2023).

Utilizing hairy roots as an innovative and sustainable method for CQD production, this study presents an efficient and eco-friendly alternative to harvesting entire plants, thereby reducing pressure on natural resources. Additionally, this method takes advantage of the benefits of hairy root cultivation in bioreactors, providing a consistent raw material source for CQDs production. It further focuses on assessing the biological activities of the green-synthesized CQDs, including their antioxidant, and antibacterial effects, along with evaluations of toxicity and biocompatibility. This research aims to leverage the unique benefits of C. asiatica in nanoform to enhance its biomedical and therapeutic applications, offering favorable biocompatibility and acceptable toxicity levels for biomedical use.

A selected hairy root line (HRL3) induced (Khalili Hassanabad et al., 2025) from the leaf segments of the stolonic stock of C. asiatica collected from Abkenar village, (37° 27′ 29″ N, 49° 20′ 33″ E, altitude of −24 m), Anzali wetland margin in Guilan Province, northern Iran (Nouri Nav et al., 2021) ( Fig. 1a) carrying the At-SS1cDNA gene (Mirjalili et al., 2011) (Fig. 1b) was used for CQDs synthesis. For instance, cultured HRL3 in a flask (250 mL) containing 50 mL of free hormones liquid Murashige and Skoog (MS) medium (pH 5.8), incubated in a shaker at 95 rpm (Unimax 1010, Heidolph Company) at 25 °C ± 2 in the dark (data not shown) was collected and dried in a freeze-drier (FD-6V, Tajhizat Sazan Pishtaz, Iran). Freez-dried hairy roots were finally powdered and homogenized by TissueLyser LT (Qiagen, Germany) and used as a precursor for CQDs synthesis.

The optimimal temperature for green synthesis of CQDs was determined by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) CQDs (Bagheri et al., 2017; Mirseyed et al., 2024). These analyses were carried out using a Mettler-Toledo TGA/DSC 2 STARe system (Mettler Toledo LLC, Columbus, OH, USA). Hairy roots powder (4.54 mg) was analyzed under synthetic air (Air Zero X50S, Air Products, Saint Quentin Fallavier, France), with the temperature range set from 25 °C to 700 °C, at a controlled heating rate of 10°C per min.

CQDs were synthesized via a simple one-pot hydrothermal process based on the method of Prathap et al. (2023), with some modifications. In shortly, 2.0 g sample of C. asiatica hairy root powder was mixed with 40 mL of distilled water and subjected to ultrasonication for 30 min at ambient temperature. The mixture was then transferred into a Teflon-lined hydrothermal reactor (150 mL), which was placed in a furnace and maintained at 190 °C for 10 h. Afterward, the reactor was allowed to cool gradually at ambient temperature. To remove larger particles, the resulting dark brown crude solution was removed from the reactor and centrifuged at 20,000 × g for 20 min. The resulting clear yellow supernatant was filtered through a syringe filtration membrane (0.22 µm) for further purification. Subsequently, the solution containing CQDs was placed in a dialysis bag with a 3 kDa cutoff and immersed in water for 24 h. The CQDs suspended in the water were stored at 4 °C, and when required, the CQDs were freeze-dried to obtain a powdered form.

The physicochemical properties of the green synthesized CQDs were evaluated using various analytical techniques. The UV–visible absorption spectrum of the CQDs in aqueous solution was recorded over a wavelength range of 200–800 nm using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2501PC, Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence spectra were analyzed using a standard bench-top Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, Varian). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern was recorded using PANalyticalX’pert pro (Holland) diffractometer equipped with a copper anode, Cu-Kα1 radiation (45 kV, 1.54056 Å; scan rate of 0.04-degree per s). IR spectra were obtained at room temperature within the wavelength range of 400–4000 cm⁻¹ using a Thermo Nicolet Nexus 470 fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) Spectrometer (Thermo Electron Corporation). The Malvern Zetasizer Nano (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK) was utilized to perform dynamic light scattering and zeta potential analyses for determining particle size, aggregation properties, and surface charge, respectively.

Bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed to examine the morphology, particle dimensions, and agglomeration behavior of the selected CQDs sample. The CQDs sample was significantly diluted in purified water. Subsequently, the suspension underwent homogenization for five min in an ultrasonic bath. Small droplets of the suspension were placed on 3 mm lacey carbon TEM grids (TED PELLA INC.) and left to air-dry for a minimum of 30 min. The CQDs on the grids were subsequently characterized using a Philips-EM 208 s microscope (FEI Co., USA) operated at an electron beam energy of 100 kV. Elemental distribution was examined through energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) mapping. To calculate the quantum yield (ɸ) of CQDs, quinine sulfate was used as a standard reference with a reported ɸ value of 0.53. During the procedure, the optical density of CQDs in water (η = 1.33) was maintained below 0.1 at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (η = 1.33) for comparison.

The emission spectra of both the CQDs sample and the reference were recorded under identical conditions at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The fluorescence quantum yield (ɸF) of CQDs was computed using the following equation.
ɸF = (AR/AS) (IS/IR) (ηS/ηR)2 ɸR

Here, the subscript ‘S’ denotes the samples, ‘R’ refers to quinine sulfate, A represents the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, I indicates the integrated emission area, and η denotes the refraction index of the solvent.

Breast cancer (MDA-MB-231) and human dermal fibroblasts (HDF) cell lines were grown in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) which included 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1 % penicillin-streptomycin. The cultures were then kept in a humidified incubator at 37°C with 5 % CO2. The cells were detached using trypsin and plated into 96-well plates at a density of 5 × 10 ³ cells per well for MDA-MB-231 and 1 × 10⁴ cells per well for HDF. The cells were permitted to adhere and multiply for 24 hours prior to treatment. After 24 hours of incubation, the culture medium was refreshed with a new medium containing various concentrations of green synthesized CQDs (0, 62.5, 125, 250, 500, 1000 μg/mL).

Control wells received medium without CQDs. Following standard procedures, treated and control cells were incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in an incubator with 5 % CO₂. Once the incubation period was complete, 20 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL in PBS) was introduced to each well and allowed to react for 4 h at 37 °C. This allowed viable cells to reduce MTT to purple formazan crystals. Following the incubation with MTT, The medium was gently discarded, and then 150 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was applied to each well to solvate the formazan crystals. The plates were slowly shaken to ensure complete solubilization. The final solution’s absorbance was evaluated by a microplate reader at 570 nm. The cell viability (%) was determined using the below formula:
Cell viability ( % ) =
Absorbance of treated cells
Absorbance of control cells
× 100
To evaluate the cytotoxic effect of the synthesized CQDs, IC50 values were determined for both cell lines tested.

Selectivity Index (SI) was measured to assess the safety or therapeutic potential of samples, particularly in the context of its effectiveness against a target (such as a cancer cells) while minimizing harm to healthy cells, using the formula:
Selectivity index ( SI ) =
IC 50
( non target )
IC 50
( target )
IC50 (non-target): The concentration of the compound that inhibits 50 % of the activity in non-target cells (e.g., healthy cells).
IC50 (target): The concentration of the compound that inhibits 50 % of the activity in target cells (e.g., cancer cells).

The hemotoxicity of CQDs was assessed by performing the hemolysis assay. Fresh red blood cells (RBCs) were isolated from healthy people’s blood by centrifugation to remove the serum. A blood sample (5 mL) was mixed with calcium- and magnesium-free phosphate-buffered saline (PBSd) (10 mL) and centrifuged at 10,000 × g for 5 min. The supernatant was discarded, and the sample underwent five additional washes before being diluted to a final volume of 50 mL in PBSd. The RBCs were then diluted 1:1 into PBSd (negative control); Triton-X-100 (positive control); and carbon dot solution (in PBSd) at varying concentrations (500, 250, 125, 62.5, 31.25, 15,6) in triplicate, repeated with at least three separate blood samples (donors). All samples were incubated with shaking at 37 °C for 3 h and subsequently centrifuged at 10,000 × g for 3 min. The absorbance of the supernatant was measured using a microplate reader at 540 nm. The hemolytic rate was subsequently computed using this formula:
Hemolysis
%
=
Ac − An
Ap − An
× 100
Here Ac, An, and Ap represent the absorbances of the CQDs, negative control, and positive control, respectively. The assay was performed in triplicate.

The 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) assay was conducted as per a modified reported method (Ali et al., 2020). DPPH solution in methanol was prepared and protected from light. CQDs were serially diluted (500, 250, 125, 62.5, 31.25, 15,6 μg mL−1), and 100 µL of each sample, Trolox as standard control, and the respective controls (blank and DPPH alone) were dispensed into the wells of a sterile 96-well microplate. To initiate the reaction, 200 µL of DPPH solution was added to each well, and the plate was incubated in the dark at room temperature for 30 min. The absorbance was then measured using a microplate reader at 517 nm. It was compared with the antioxidant activity of various concentrations of standard control. All experiments were performed in triplicate. The radical scavenging activity (RSA) was determined based on this formula:
RSA ( % ) =
Ab ( C )
− Ab ( S )
Ab ( C )
× 100
Ab (C) and Ab (S) represent the absorbance of the control and samples, respectively, while RSA denotes the radical scavenging activity.

Escherichia coli (ATCC 25922), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (ATCC 27853), both Gram-negative bacteria, and Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 25923), a Gram-positive bacterium, chosen due to their medical significance and relevance to the experiment. One of the techniques employed in this research to examine the antibacterial activity of CQDs was the disc diffusion method. In this assay, bacterial inhibition was evaluated by creating a lawn culture of each bacterial strain. This was performed by evenly spreading 100 μL of the bacterial suspension (adjusted to the 0.5 McFarland standard) across the surface of Mueller-Hinton Agar plates via a sterile swab. Disks impregnated with the test substances were placed in the prepared medium. After incubating at 37 °C for 24 h, the diameter of the inhibition zone around each disk was measured and recorded. Each disk diffusion test was independently replicated thrice (Howe and Andrews, 2012).

To determine the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC), E. coli, P. aeruginosa, and S. aureus were incubated overnight at 37°C in Mueller-Hinton Broth (MHB). A spectrophotometer (UV-2501PC, Shimadzu, Japan) was utilized to calibrate the bacterial cultures to turbidity matching the 0.5 McFarland standard, or roughly 1 × 108 colony forming unit (CFU)/mL. Serial dilutions of green synthesized CQDs were prepared to obtain concentrations ranging. 32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1, 0.5, 0.25, 0.125, 0.062 mg/mL. 100 μL of each bacterial suspension was added to the wells of a sterile 96-well microplate containing 100 μL of different concentrations of CQDs. Control wells contained bacteria without CQDs, and wells with MHB only served as sterility controls. The plates were then incubated 37 °C for 24 h. Following incubation, bacterial growth was evaluated by recording the absorbance at 600 nm with a microplate reader. MIC was indicated by a lack of turbidity or absorbance.

Statistical analysis was conducted using GraphPad Prism software (version 8 for Windows, GraphPad Software, La Jolla, California, USA). Data from triplicates of each experiment were subjected to one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with a post-hoc Tukey test or two-way ANOVA with Tukey’s multiple comparisons test to assess variation and statistical significance between groups, as indicated by F values (p < 0.05). The results are presented as mean ± standard deviation.

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and derivative thermogravimetry (DTG) were utilized to assess the thermal steadiness and decomposition behavior of dried hairy root samples, aimed at identifying the optimal temperature for the synthesis of CQDs. TGA-DSC analysis of the precursor material revealed that decomposition began within the temperature range of 124 °C to 382 °C. The most rapid weight loss occurs between 180 °C and 210 °C, according to the TGA diagram ( Fig. 2a), which implies the critical range for efficient carbonization and the formation of CQDs (Bagheri et al., 2017; Mirseyed et al., 2024). To further refine the synthesis conditions, experiments were conducted at 180 °C, 190 °C, and 200 °C. Among these, CQDs synthesized at 190 °C exhibited the highest fluorescence intensity, indicating that this temperature is optimal for producing CQDs with superior optical properties.

The DSC data (Fig. 2b) provide further support for these findings by revealing two primary thermal events: the first decomposition stage between 150 °C and 300 °C, and a second, more substantial event from 300 °C to 500 °C. The initial stage corresponds to the breakdown of volatile components and lighter organic materials, aligning with the TGA-detected weight loss in the 180 °C to 210 °C range. At 190 °C the crystallization onset temperature is observed, as confirmed by the higher fluorescence intensity of the synthesized CQDs at this temperature, compared with others. The precursor-to-solvent weight ratio, and synthesis duration were optimized based on a comprehensive literature survey, between 3 % and 8 %, and 6 h to 12 h, respectively. Higher and more stable fluorescence intensity of the synthesized CQDs was selected as the desirable outcome. Following this evaluation, a precursor-to-solvent ratio of 5 % was determined to be optimal, with a synthesis time of 10 h identified as the most suitable for achieving the desired outcomes.

The average particle size of CQDs was determined using dynamic light scattering (DLS) and TEM. The TEM results demonstrate that the synthesized CQDs exhibit a spherical shape, confirming their uniform morphology and nanoscale dimensions ( Fig. 3a). The DLS particle size distribution shows that the CQDs possessed an average diameter of 1.07 ± 0.22 nm (Fig. 3b). The histogram of particle size distribution is illustrated in Fig. 3c, in which the majority of particle diameters fall within the range of 0.83–1.2 nm. The observed uniformity in particle size was confirmed both in TEM and DLS analyses.

Previous studies have reported varying particle sizes for CQDs synthesized via green methods from different biomass sources. For instance, CQDs derived from Hibiscus rosa-sinensis L. (Yalshetti et al., 2024), wheat and oat flour (Kuppusamy et al., 2024), Curcuma zedoaria Roxb. (Zhang et al., 2024), and cassava (Manihot esculenta) waste peels (Surendran et al., 2024) have exhibited average sizes ranging from approximately 3 to 12 nm, depending on the synthesis conditions and precursor materials. These variations in particle size significantly impact the optical, structural, and biological properties of the CQDs. Compared to these studies, the CQDs synthesized in this work, with an average size of 1.07 ± 0.22 nm, are notably smaller. This reduced size is expected to enhance their biocompatibility, cellular uptake, and fluorescence properties, making them especially advantageous for biomedical applications. The small and uniform size ensures more consistent biological responses, such as improved drug loading, increased stability, and greater efficiency in targeted therapies.

The ultra-small average size of 1.07 ± 0.22 nm for C.asiatica hairy roots CQDs plays a crucial role in enhancing their biological properties. Due to their small size, these CQDs allow for efficient internalization into cells, which is crucial for bioimaging, drug delivery, and targeted therapies. Furthermore, the high surface-area-to-volume ratio increases interactions with biological molecules, which boosts their drug-loading capacity and catalytic activity. The quantum confinement effect at this small scale enhances their photoluminescence, making them suitable for imaging and sensing applications. The narrow size distribution ( ± 0.22 nm) ensures uniform biological responses, minimizing variability in cellular interactions and improving the reproducibility of therapeutic outcomes.

This highly uniform size also improves the dispersion of CQDs in biological environments, potentially reducing cytotoxicity and enhancing biocompatibility. These features make the ultra-small CQDs synthesized from C. asiatica hairy roots highly promising for various biomedical applications, including drug delivery systems and medical imaging (Jelinek, 2016; Wang et al., 2019a).

The apparent zeta potential value was found to be 17.0 ± 4.7 mV, indicating a moderately positive surface charge. This charge can be attributed to the presence of functional groups such as amine or hydroxyl groups introduced during the green synthesis process (Hunter, 2013). These functional groups contribute to the colloidal stability of the CQDs by enhancing their dispersibility in aqueous media, though the zeta potential value lower than the ± 30 mV threshold is typically associated with highly stable colloids (Předota et al., 2016). While the CQDs exhibit reasonable short-term stability due to electrostatic repulsion, their long-term stability in suspension may be limited. The relatively low variability in the measured zeta potential (standard deviation of 4.7 mV) suggests consistent surface functionalization across the particles. Nevertheless, for applications requiring extended colloidal stability, surface modification strategies aimed at increasing the zeta potential may be beneficial. Despite this, the current zeta potential is sufficient for immediate applications such as bioimaging or sensing, where short-term stability is adequate.

XRD pattern of the CQDs is represented in Fig. 5a, which exhibits two sharp diffraction peaks at 2θ = 28.28°, 40.4°, indicating the presence of well-crystallized phases with specific lattice planes that contribute to these distinct reflections. In the range between 20 and 30 degrees, the (002) peaks and between 40 and 50 degrees (101) graphitic carbon crystalline phases are remarkable (Klug and Alexander, 1974; Qiu et al., 2019). Regardless of the synthesis technique, or the sources used as precursors, rather broader XRD peaks occur because the crystal domains in this range are not limited to graphite carbon (002), but some other amorphous cross-linked polymer skeletons exist in the structures. These amorphous skeletons are known to contribute to some of the unique properties of the CQDs, such as their optical behavior, solubility, and potential for functionalization in various applications (Gates-Rector and Blanton, 2019; Tao et al., 2017).

In the UV–visible spectra of CQDs, two prominent absorption peaks were observed at 240 nm and 310 nm with a tail spread over the visible region ( Fig. 4a). The peak at 240 nm is indicative of π-π* transitions, commonly associated with the conjugated π-electron systems within the CQDs. It can be elucidated that electrons transit from the π orbital to CN bonds. This transition reflects the presence of aromatic structures or conjugated bonds, which are integral to the electronic configuration of the CQDs. The absorption peak at 310 nm can be attributed to n-π* transitions, which are indicative of electronic transitions from C–O or CO bonds to π* orbitals, and it is often associated with the presence of carbonyl or other functional groups in CQDs (Cui et al., 2021; Daniel, 2023).

The photoluminescence behavior of the synthesized CQDs was analyzed over a range of excitation wavelengths from 310 nm to 400 nm, with increments of 10 nm (Fig. 4b). Notably, the maximum emission intensity was recorded at excitation wavelengths of 340 nm and 350 nm. Accordingly, the emission peaks were observed at 418 nm and 428 nm, respectively. Within the excitation range of 310–340 nm, the emission peaks were observed in the 400–420 nm range, which corresponds to the emission of violet light. This indicates that at lower excitation wavelengths, the energy levels involved in the electronic transitions within the CQDs are higher, resulting in shorter wavelength emissions.

The violet emission is typically associated with higher energy transitions, often linked to surface states or smaller sized domains within the CQDs. As the excitation wavelength increased from 350 to 400 nm, the emission peaks shifted to a longer wavelength range, specifically between 428 and 470 nm, corresponding to blue light emission. This redshift in the emission peak with increasing excitation wavelength is a characteristic of CQDs and can be attributed to the presence of multiple emissive sites or different sizes of nanoparticles within the sample. The longer excitation wavelengths probe lower energy transitions, likely involving different surface states or larger particles, leading to the observed blue emission (You et al., 2024).

These findings highlight the tunable nature of the photoluminescence properties of these green synthesized CQDs, which is crucial for various applications such as bioimaging, where specific emission wavelengthes can be targeted for different imaging requirements. The ability to emit in both violet and blue regions of the spectrum under different excitation conditions also underscores the versatility of these CQDs in potential optoelectronic and sensing applications. The excitation-dependent emission behavior observed here aligns with previous studies on CQDs, which have reported similar tunability in emission properties based on the excitation wavelength. This tunability is largely influenced by the quantum confinement effect and the presence of various functional groups on the surface of the CQDs, which can modify the electronic structure and, consequently, the emission characteristics (Mai et al., 2020).

The FTIR spectrum of CQDs displays various absorption features ( Fig. 5b). The broad peak at 3463 cm⁻¹ in the FTIR spectrum indicates the presence of hydrogen-bonded O–H stretching vibrations. This broadening occurs due to the variety of hydrogen bonding environments, which weakens and varies the O–H bond strengths and lengths, leading to a range of vibrational frequencies. The small peak at 3747 cm⁻¹ suggests the presence of free O–H stretching vibrations from hydroxyl groups that are not involved in strong hydrogen bonding.

This peak is typically associated with isolated or less extensively hydrogen-bonded hydroxyl groups in the sample, indicating that these groups are present but less engaged in networked interactions compared with those contributing to broader peaks (Knop et al., 2011). In the context of the green synthesized CQDs from C. asiatica hairy roots, the broad peak at 3463 cm⁻¹ and the small broad peak at 3747 cm⁻¹ suggest the hydrophilicity and stability of the CQDs, enhancing their suitability for various biological applications.

The sharp peak at 1664 cm⁻¹ corresponds to CO stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of carbonyl groups, which may improve the reactivity and functionality of the CQDs. The peak at 1588 cm⁻¹ is associated with CC stretching vibrations, suggesting aromatic ring structures, as admitted by XRD results described in the following (Cao et al., 2022; Wong, 2015). The peaks at 1385 cm⁻¹ and 1286 cm⁻¹ correspond to C–H bending vibrations and C–N stretching vibrations, reflecting the presence of alkanes, methyl or methylene groups, and aromatic amine or aromatic ester groups, respectively.

Finally, the peak observed at 1069 cm⁻¹ is commonly associated with C–O stretching vibrations, particularly in functional groups such as alcohols, ethers, and polysaccharides. In the CQDs derived from C. asiatica transgenic hairy roots, this peak likely reflects the presence of glycosidic linkages (C–O–C), indicative of polysaccharides or other plant-derived saccharide structures. This finding suggests that components of the plant cell wall, including cellulose and hemicellulose, contribute to the surface functionalization of the CQDs. Together, these peaks highlight the diverse functional groups on the surface of the CQDs, which impact their chemical reactivity, stability, and suitability for various applications.

Plants rich in carbon-containing compounds, such as carbohydrates, proteins, and phenolic compounds, provide ideal precursors for CQD formation. These biomolecules not only provide the carbon backbone but also introduce heteroatoms (e.g., N, S, P) and functional groups (–OH, –COOH, –NH₂) that critically influence the physicochemical and electronic properties of CQDs. These functional groups act as nucleation sites during carbonization and influence the surface chemistry and the resulting CQD architecture (Omar et al., 2022). For instance, the bio-waste lignin yield N-CQDs with graphite-like structure, strong hydrophilicity, tiny particle size (6.9 nm), and bright green fluorescence with a QY of approximately 22.2 % (Ren et al., 2024). Moreover, the N-CQDs exhibit high fluorescence stability under the conditions of a range of pH (5–11). Jia et al. (2024) demonstrated that carbohydrate-rich residue of birch (Betula L.) obtained from lignin-first strategy are ideal precursors for synthesizing CQD with high mass yield and QY by combining solvothermal treatment and chemical oxidation methods, offering a novel approach for the utilization of whole biomass components following the lignin-first strategy. In another study, Li et al. (2022) synthesized CQDs from wasted enzymatic hydrolysis lignin catalyzed by organic acids (e.g. citric, malic, and oxalic acids) by a hydrothermal technology for UV shielding and antioxidant fluorescent flexible film. The synthesized CQDs resulted in an increase in the fluorescence intensity and effective QY up to 500 % and 4.5 %, respectively, displaying a narrower size distribution and a mean particle size of ca. 3.1 nm.

A study by Raveendran et al. (2019) demonstrated that mint leaf-derived CQDs, rich in chlorophyll and antioxidants, exhibited a QY of 7.64 %, attributed to surface passivation by polyphenols. The presence of hydroxyl (-OH) and carboxyl (-COOH) functional groups facilitated strong binding with Fe³ ⁺ ions, leading to fluorescence quenching, while the removal of Fe³ ⁺ by ascorbic acid restored the luminescence.

The use of transgenic HRs of C. asiatica carrying the At-SQS1 gene represents a novel and highly effective approach for the synthesis of CQDs. The SQS gene plays a critical role in the biosynthesis of squalene, a key intermediate in the triterpenoid pathway. So, by overexpressing this gene, the transgenic HRs exhibit enhanced metabolic activity, leading to increased production of triterpenoids, phenolic compounds, and other carbon-rich SMs. These compounds serve as excellent precursors for CQDs synthesis, as they provide a high concentration of carbon sources and functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups) that facilitate the carbonization and surface functionalization processes. This metabolic enhancement likely contributes to the higher yield and superior optical properties of the CQDs synthesized from transgenic HRs compared to those from wild-type plant materials.

Although a direct comparison with CQDs synthesized from the plant wild-type HRs was not included in the present study, our previous work (Khalili Hassanabad et al., 2025) clearly demonstrated that At-SQS1-expressing transgenic HRs produce significantly higher levels of centellosides than wild-type HRs. This prior finding provides strong evidence that the improved biosynthetic capacity conferred by At-SQS1 likely contributes to the superior efficiency and quality of CQDs synthesized in this system. Moreover, although exogenous application of carbon-rich compounds to wild-type roots could potentially enhance metabolite levels to some extent, such interventions do not replicate the systemic and stable upregulation of secondary metabolism achieved through genetic transformation. Therefore, the enhanced performance observed in our CQD synthesis is most likely attributable to the intrinsic metabolic reprogramming induced by At-SQS1 overexpression.

The peak at 3216 cm⁻¹ is typically associated with N-H stretching, from amide groups present in proteins or peptides. The peak at 2929 cm−1 corresponds to aliphatic C–H stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of aliphatic hydrocarbon chains. The peaks around 2024 cm−1 and 2141 cm−1 are assigned to strong N = CN stretching or weak CC stretching. The weak peak at 814 cm−1 indicates out-of-plane C-H bending vibrations, suggesting the presence of aromatic rings within the CQDs, which contribute to their stability, electronic properties, and potential interactions in various applications (Chunduri et al., 2017; De and Karak, 2013).

The EDX analysis of the green-synthesized CQDs derived from C. asiatica transgenic hairy roots revealed the following elemental composition: 55.94 % carbon, 2.53 % nitrogen, 25.1 % oxygen, 11.62 % potassium, 3.14 % chlorine, 0.43 % sulfur, and 0.2 % phosphorus (Fig. 5g). The high carbon content confirms the successful formation of CQDs, as carbon is the primary structural component of these nanoparticles. The substantial presence of oxygen admits the dominant incorporation of oxygen-containing functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl groups, as confirmed by FTIR results. These groups are essential for enhancing the hydrophilicity, stability, and reactivity of the CQDs, which can be advantageous in applications like bioimaging and drug delivery.

The 2.53 % nitrogen content of CQDs, which possibly exists due to the proteins or amino acids of the plant source, can improve the electronic properties and quantum yield of the CQDs, making them more suitable for optoelectronic and bioimaging applications. The presence of 11.62 % potassium might aid in stabilizing the CQDs structure and could be attributed to the residual elements from the plant material. The chlorine (3.14 %) and sulfur (0.43 %) content of the CQDS, introduced from the plant source, and also contribute to additional functional properties, such as enhancing antimicrobial activity or modifying surface chemistry for targeted interactions (Won et al., 2013). In conclusion, the EDX analysis highlights the complex elemental composition of the green-synthesized CQDs, with carbon and oxygen as the predominant elements. These elements and their associated functional groups endow the CQDs with unique properties, making them suitable for diverse applications in biomedical, environmental, and industrial fields.

To evaluate the biocompatibility of the CQDs, MTT and hemolysis assays were conducted. The cytotoxic effects of CQDs, the methanolic extract (ME), and the water extract (AQE) from C. asiatica hairy roots were assessed on human dermal fibroblasts (HDF) and MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells through IC50 determination and the selectivity index (SI) ( Table 1). CQDs exhibited a high IC50 of 14,697.9 µg/mL on HDF cells and a much lower value of 1057.85 µg/mL on MDA-MB-231 cells, resulting in a high SI of 13.87. This indicates strong selectivity towards cancer cells, making CQDs a candidate for anticancer therapies. In contrast, ME displayed IC50 values of 6812.7 µg/mL for HDF and 14,153 µg/mL for MDA-MB-231 cells, yielding a low SI of 0.48, which suggests poor selectivity and greater toxicity to normal cells.

The MTT assay results further highlight the potential of green-synthesized CQDs in terms of biocompatibility and therapeutic efficacy. CQDs demonstrated excellent biocompatibility in fibroblasts, with cell viability exceeding 90.62 % at a concentration of 125 µg/mL and remaining at 74.25 % even at 1000 µg/mL ( Fig. 6b). This suggests that these CQDs are relatively non-toxic to healthy cells, even at higher concentrations, making them suitable for biomedical applications where safety and minimal adverse effects on normal tissues are critical. The high biocompatibility of CQDs positions them as ideal candidates for applications such as drug delivery systems and bioimaging, where preserving the integrity of normal cells is essential (Nocito et al., 2021).

In contrast, the MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells exhibited a dose-dependent cytotoxic response to CQDs (Fig. 6b). The IC50 of 1057.85 µg/mL indicates that this concentration reduced cell viability by 50 %, with a more pronounced cytotoxic effect at higher concentrations. For instance, at 500 µg/mL, cell viability was 57.17 %, and at 1000 µg/mL, it further decreased to 50.7 %.

The observed IC50 demonstrates promising cytotoxic effects on cancer cells, supporting their role in combination cancer therapies with enhanced safety for normal cells (Jia et al., 2020; Shen et al., 2022). Nevertheless, for emerging nanomaterials such as CQDs, higher IC50 values can still be acceptable due to their lower toxicity to normal cells, biocompatibility, and potential for functionalization to improve cancer cell targeting (Boakye-Yiadom et al., 2019). The green-synthesized CQDs derived from C. asiatica hairy roots likely contain surface functional groups that are influenced by the bioactive compounds naturally present in C. asiatica, such as asiaticoside, madecassoside, and various flavonoids. These functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups, are known for enhancing the bioactivity of CQDs, particularly in cancer therapeutics.

The presence of these bioactive compounds on the surface of the CQDs can improve their interaction with biological targets, potentially contributing to selective cytotoxicity toward cancer cells while maintaining low toxicity to normal cells. The natural compounds from C. asiatica have been reported for their antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and anticancer properties, which could further enhance the therapeutic potential of the CQDs.

The IC50 value highlights the potential of these CQDs to selectively target cancer cells, and their biocompatibility and surface functionality further enhance their therapeutic applicability. The relatively high IC50 suggests moderate cytotoxicity, but this can be balanced by leveraging the unique properties of CQDs, including their biocompatibility and surface functionalization. Functionalized CQDs can act synergistically with other chemotherapeutic agents, reducing the necessary therapeutic dose and improving cancer cell selectivity. Additionally, the nanostructure of these CQDs allows for targeted drug delivery, concentrating therapeutic agents at tumor sites and minimizing off-target effects. Therefore, while the IC50 alone may not reflect potent anticancer activity, the functionalized surfaces and multifunctional capabilities of C. asiatica-derived CQDs position them as promising candidates to enhance the efficacy of existing cancer treatments.

The hemolysis assay was conducted to evaluate the biocompatibility of green-synthesized CQDs derived from C. asiatica hairy roots at concentrations ranging from 15.6 to 1000 μg/mL. The results indicated that the hemolysis percentage remained relatively low across all concentrations, reflecting minimal disruption to red blood cells (RBCs) and demonstrating high biocompatibility ( Fig. 7). At the lowest concentration of 15.6 μg/mL, the hemolysis percentage was 0.31 %, indicating negligible hemolytic activity. As the concentration increased, a slight rise in hemolysis was observed, with 0.42 % at 31.25 μg/mL and 1.04 % at 62.5 μg/mL, confirming that even at moderate concentrations, the CQDs maintained excellent biocompatibility. At 125 μg/mL, the hemolysis percentage remained low at 1.36 %, further affirming the safety profile of the CQDs.

At higher concentrations of 250 μg/mL and 500 μg/mL, hemolysis values increased slightly to 1.67 % and 2.29 %, respectively. At the maximum concentration of 1000 μg/mL, the hemolysis percentage reached 2.51 %, still well below the critical 5 % threshold, which is considered safe for biomedical applications. Overall, the hemolysis percentages across all concentrations were significantly below the 5 % limit, which is the standard for cytocompatibility in hemolysis assays. These results suggest that the CQDs are highly biocompatible with minimal hemolytic effects, even at elevated concentrations. Consequently, CQDs show promise as a safe candidate for biomedical applications such as drug delivery or bioimaging, where interaction with blood components is critical.

The antioxidant capabilities of CQDs, ME, and AQE derived from C. asiatica hairy roots were evaluated using DPPH radical scavenging activity at different concentrations (15.6, 31.25, 62.5, 125, 250, 500 μg/mL) ( Table 2). The standard control (Ctrl) group was trolax which demonstrated the highest radical scavenging activity across all concentrations, with a maximum of 90.86 % at 500 μg/mL, followed by the CQDs, ME, and AQE groups (Fig. 6a). At the lowest concentration of 15.6 μg/mL, the scavenging activity was minimal across all samples, with 7.772 % in Ctrl, 6.368 % in CQDs, 5.801 % in ME, and 3.886 % in AQE. As the concentration increased, the scavenging activity improved in all samples, reflecting a dose-dependent relationship.

At 250 μg/ mL, Ctrl exhibited a high activity of 77.06 %, while CQDs reached 67.71 %. However, the ME and AQE samples showed considerably lower activities of 37.21 % and 25.56 %, respectively. At the highest concentration of 500 μg/mL, the scavenging activities in the CQDs (83.52 %) and Ctrl (90.86 %) groups were relatively close, indicating the superior antioxidative properties of CQDs compared with ME (52.54 %) and AQE (32.08 %).

The IC50 values, which represent the concentration required to achieve 50 % DPPH radical scavenging activity, further highlight the differences in antioxidant efficacy. Ctrl and CQDs demonstrated relatively low IC50 values of 140.9 μg/mL and 146.5 μg/mL, respectively, indicating strong antioxidant activity. In contrast, ME and AQE exhibited much higher IC50 values of 430 μg/mL and 732 μg/mL, suggesting significantly weaker antioxidative potential.

The CQDs, despite being slightly less effective than Ctrl, still demonstrate promising antioxidant potential, especially when considering their close IC50 values. On the other hand, the markedly higher IC50 values for ME and AQE suggest that these extracts are less efficient in combating oxidative stress. The relatively low scavenging activity in AQE across all concentrations suggests that this sample may have limited use as an antioxidant, whereas CQDs could serve as a potent alternative to traditional antioxidants.

In summary, the findings highlight the potential application of CQDs in antioxidant formulations, providing nearly comparable efficacy to standard control, and suggest that ME and AQE, while less effective, may still offer some antioxidative benefits at higher concentrations. The potential antioxidant properties of CQDs are likely due to the presence of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and other functional groups on their surface, which can donate electrons or hydrogen atoms to neutralize free radicals. The concentration-dependent antioxidant activity observed in this study underscores the potential of green synthesized CQDs from C. asiatica hairy roots as effective natural antioxidants. Their antioxidant capacity highlights their potential application in biomedical fields, such as in developing antioxidant therapies, preserving oxidative-sensitive materials, and incorporating them into nutraceuticals.

The antioxidant activity of CQDs synthesized from C. asiatica hairy roots can be attributed to the diverse functional groups introduced during the hydrothermal carbonization process. insights from the literature provide a plausible explanation for the formation and functionalization of CQDs from C. asiatica. The primary constituents of C. asiatica, such as triterpenes (asiatic acid and asiaticoside), polyacetylenes, phenolic compounds, carbohydrates, and amino acids, undergo complex transformations under hydrothermal conditions. During the carbonization process, carbohydrates present in these compounds decompose into monosaccharides like glucose and fructose, which further break down into soluble products, including furfural compounds (Thokchom et al., 2023).

These intermediate products are crucial for forming the carbon framework of the CQDs. The phenolic compounds and acids present in C. asiatica can play a significant role in the antioxidant properties of the CQDs. The hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CQDs, derived from the phenolic constituents, contribute significantly to their antioxidant activity. Similarly, the carboxyl groups originating from triterpenes like asiatic acid enhance the radical scavenging capacity by providing additional sites for hydrogen atom donation.

Polyacetylenes and amino acids further enrich the surface chemistry of the CQDs. Polyacetylenes can undergo polymerization and condensation, leading to the formation of aromatic clusters, which are essential for the nucleation of carbon dots. These aromatic clusters, formed from the soluble polymers, contribute to the stability and electronic properties of the CQDs. Amino acids, with their amine groups, add nitrogen-containing functionalities to the CQDs, which can enhance their electron-donating capabilities, further boosting the antioxidant activity. To sum up, the capacity of CQDs synthesized from hairy roots of C. asiatica to neutralize free radicals can be attributed to hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amine groups, as well as aromatic structures derived from phenolic chemicals, triterpenes, polyethylenes, and amino acids. The complex surface chemistry in CQDs makes them suitable for a wide range of industrial and pharmaceutical applications.

The antimicrobial activity of CQDs, ME, and AQE derived from the transgenic line of C. asiatica hairy roots was tested against 3 different bacterial strains E. coli (ATCC 25922), P. aeruginosa (ATCC 27853), (Gram-negative bacteria), and S. aureus (ATCC 25923), (Gram-positive bacteria) ( Table 3). The disk diffusion method was employed on MH agar plates, with each disk containing 0.5 mg of CQDs, ME, or AQE ( Fig. 8). Results indicated that neither ME nor AQE exhibited any observable zone of inhibition at the tested concentration (0.5 mg/mL). In contrast, CQDs demonstrated some antimicrobial potential against all tested bacteria.

These findings align with previous research conducted by Wong and Ramli (2021), which reported limited antimicrobial activity of aqueous extract and methanolic extracts of C. asiatica against E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus. Their study revealed that the aqueous extract exhibited a high MIC of 250 mg/mL for S. aureus, with no detectable MIC established for E. coli O157:H7. Similarly, the methanolic extract did not produce detectable MIC values for either bacterium, suggesting minimal antimicrobial efficacy in low concentrations (Wong and Ramli, 2021).

In terms of MIC values, CQDs exhibited the lowest MICs across the three bacterial strains tested: 0.25 mg/mL for S. aureus, 1 mg/mL for P. aeruginosa, and 2 mg/mL for E. coli. These values highlight CQDs’ potent antimicrobial activity, particularly against S. aureus, which might be due to the greater susceptibility of Gram-positive bacteria to the membrane disruption effects of CQDs. In contrast, the methanolic and aqueous extracts showed significantly weaker antibacterial efficacy, with MIC values of 32 mg/mL for E. coli, 16 mg/mL for S. aureus ME, and 32 mg/mL for S. aureus AQE. For P. aeruginosa, both ME and AQE had MIC values exceeding 32 mg/mL, indicating minimal antibacterial activity. This trend aligns with the findings in the review by Ghirardello et al. (2021), which highlights the unique properties of CDs including ROS generation and bacterial membrane interaction that likely contribute to their enhanced antimicrobial activity. The significantly higher MIC values for ME and AQE suggest that, while these extracts contain bioactive compounds, their antimicrobial mechanisms are far less effective than the more advanced nanomaterial-based CQDs.

The antibacterial activity of CQDs is strongly linked to their core structure and surface functional groups. Positively charged CQDs interact with negatively charged bacterial cell walls via electrostatic forces, leading to membrane disruption and apoptosis (Bing et al., 2016). The CQDs synthesized in this study, with a moderately positive surface charge (+17 ± 4.7 mV), enhance these electrostatic interactions, promoting the adsorption of CQDs onto bacterial surfaces and facilitating membrane disruption.

The incorporation of nitrogen, as confirmed by EDX and FTIR analysis, further supports this antibacterial mechanism by introducing nitrogen-containing functional groups, such as amines, which enhance electrostatic interactions and reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation. Additionally, the antibacterial properties of these CQDs are influenced by the metabolites of C. asiatica used during their synthesis. During the hydrothermal carbonization process, metabolites such as carbohydrates, phenolics, and triterpenes from C. asiatica undergo transformations that introduce functional groups like hydroxyls and carboxyls onto the CQDs surface (Thokchom et al., 2023).

These functional groups contribute to enhanced ROS production, membrane disruption, and overall antibacterial efficacy. Thus, both the surface charge and complex surface chemistry derived from C. asiatica metabolites play crucial roles in the potent antibacterial activity of the synthesized CQDs.

Carbon quantum dots can be fabricated utilizing two sources, either from chemical or green precursors. In recent years, researchers have focused more on green nontoxic and low-cost carbon precursors for great-quality and large-scale synthesis of CQDs. Various green precursors including plant parts (flowers, fruits, stems, seeds, leaves, vegetables), crop residues (wheat straw, palm kernel shell, sago waste), bacteria/fungi species (algal blooms, mushroom, Shewanella oneidensis, microalgae biochar), and waste products (expired milk, tea powders, coal powder, polypropylene plastic waste) have been employed as a carbon precursor for the production of CQDs (Omar et al., 2022). Green precursors based on plant parts and crop residues, named biomass, are not only varied and broadly distributed, with a great content of oxygen and carbon, but also renewable, non-toxic, highly biodegradable, and abundant in sources. To date, numerous biomass sources have been used as excellent raw substances for the production of CQDs (Dong et al., 2024). Zhou et al. (2021) produced blue-emitting CQDs in a hydrothermal method from ethylenediamine and quinoa saponin powder. Using combustion method, Jagannathan et al. (2021) synthesized CQDs with white emissive from corn grains. Nguyen et al. (2022) prepared blue highly fluorescent CQDs from banana peels by a hydrothermal process.

The synthesis, properties, and applications of CQDs derived from some medicinal plants are summarized in Table 4. This comparative analysis highlights the unique aspects of CQDs synthesized from C. asiatica HRs. The findings underscore the novelty and advantages of this study in comparison to previous reports. While most reported CQDs are synthesized from aerial parts, leaves, fruits, or roots of medicinal plants, this study is the first to utilize C. asiatica HRs culture as a sustainable and scalable source for CQD production. Hairy root cultures offer a genetically stable and eco-friendly biomass, eliminating the need for wild plant harvesting and contributing to the conservation of genetic resources. This innovative approach addresses the growing demand for sustainable methods in nanomaterial synthesis.

The CQDs from C. asiatica HRs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method at 190 °C for 10 h. This method is consistent with other hydrothermal approaches used for CQDs synthesis from plants such as Perilla frutescens, Osmanthus fragrans, and Ginkgo biloba (Table 4). However, the use of hairy root cultures as a source material is a groundbreaking aspect of this work, offering a scalable and environmentally friendly alternative to traditional plant sources.

The QY of CQDs derived from C. asiatica HRs is 9.7 %, which is higher than that of CQDs from P. frutescens (9.01 %), G. biloba (6.16 %), and Puerariae lobatae Radix (3.2 %). Although the QY is lower than that of O. fragrans (21.9 %), and Panax notoginseng (18.41 %), it remains competitive, particularly considering the sustainable nature of the source material (Table 4).

The CQDs synthesized from C. asiatica HRs exhibit an ultra-small average particle size of 1.07 nm, with a narrow size distribution ranging from 0.82 to 1.2 nm. This is significantly smaller than most reported CQDs, such as those from P. notoginseng (6.66 nm) and Gardenia jasminoides (2.08 nm). The small and uniform size of these CQDs enhances their potential for applications in bioimaging and other biomedical fields.

The CQDs derived from C. asiatica HRs demonstrate promising potential for anticancer, antibacterial, and antioxidant applications, as well as bioimaging. This broad range of applications is comparable to or even exceeds the functional applications of CQDs from other medicinal plants, such as P. lobatae Radix (anti-hyperuricemic and anti-inflammatory) and Annona squamosa (anti-inflammatory and anti-angiogenesis).

For the first time, this study reports the synthesis of CQDs from the hairy roots culture of C. asiatica, an ancient medicinal plant, using a one-pot hydrothermal method. Hairy roots culture offer a sustainable and scalable platform for biomass production, promoting rapid growth and contributing to genetic stability under controlled conditions. This method protects wild plant populations, preserves genetic diversity, and ensures consistent bioactive compound quality while contributing directly to global sustainability efforts. By promoting eco-friendly and responsible plant harvesting practices, it aligns with the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (UN SDGs), particularly SDG 12 (Responsible Consumption and Production) and SDG 15 (Life on Land).

Uniformly dispersed spherical CQDs synthesized from hairy roots culture of C. asiatica, with a blue fluorescent appearance, showed the presence of functional groups including hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, and amino groups on their surface, which determines their functional biological properties such as antioxidant and antibacterial capabilities. The positive surface charge enables the CQDs to interact effectively with bacterial membranes, positioning them as candidates for antimicrobial applications. C. asiatica hairy root derived CQDs can be integrated into hydrogels, films, or coatings for wound dressings, offering localized antibacterial effects and facilitating the monitoring of wound healing or bacterial colonization through their fluorescent properties. In synergistic therapy with antibiotics, C. asiatica hairy roots-derived CQDs can function as carriers, which may facilitate antibiotic penetration into bacterial cells and potentially reduce the required dosage. Moreover, these functionalized CQDs can help overcome bacterial resistance mechanisms, such as efflux pumps and biofilm formation. The selectivity index observed in this study indicates that CQDs synthesized from C. asiatica hairy roots exhibit cytotoxic effects toward cancer cells, though with room for further enhancement. This moderate selectivity index provides a stepping stone for utilizing CQDs in multimodal cancer therapy, as a promising tool in modern oncology. Building on this foundation, future research can focus on enhancing their targeted delivery capabilities through surface functionalization. Moreover, their intrinsic photoluminescence and ability to generate ROS under light exposure suggest their potential for applications such as photodynamic therapy. Future research can focus on optimizing targeted delivery strategies and exploring broader biomedical applications, which may contribute to broader health-related goals and support future innovation in biomedical applications.

Taheri Zohreh: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data interpretation, Writing – original draft. Mirjalili Mohammad Hossein: Supervision, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Shahsavarani Hosein: Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Ghassempour Alireza: Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Rahmandoust Moones: Supervision, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing.
